name: Early Blight
symptoms: Concentric brown rings on lower leaves (bullseye spots)
Yellow halos surrounding lesions
Premature leaf drop starting from bottom branches
cause: Fungal pathogen favored by warm, humid conditions and heavy dew.
lifecycle: Spreads via airborne spores in 7–10 day cycles, overwintering in plant debris.

organic.safety: Apply in late evening to avoid burning leaves.
Apply at first sign of disease; wash hands after application.

prevention: Rotate crops with non-solanaceous plants every 3 years.
Remove and destroy all infected plant debris at the end of the season.
Use drip irrigation to keep leaves completely dry.

name: Late Blight
symptoms: Dark water-soaked spots expanding rapidly on leaves
White downy growth on leaf undersides in humid conditions
Large dark brown lesions on fruits
cause: Water mold favored by cool, wet weather and high humidity.
lifecycle: Extremely rapid lifecycle producing millions of wind-dispersed spores.

organic.safety: Full coverage is crucial; apply to both leaf sides.

chemical.safety: Observe standard spray precautions and local regulations.

prevention: Select certified disease-free transplants.
Avoid overhead irrigation entirely.
Remove any volunteer tomato plants or nightshade weeds.

name: Healthy
symptoms: Green, vibrant foliage with no visible spots or discoloration.
cause: Optimal care, proper watering, and sufficient nutrients.

prevention: Continue regular weeding and checkups.
Apply organic mulch to retain soil moisture.
Support plants with stakes or cages to keep leaves off the ground.

name: Early Blight
symptoms: Small, dark, circular spots on older leaves
Spots develop concentric rings resembling targets
Yellowing of surrounding tissue
cause: Fungal infection thriving on stressed or aging plants.
lifecycle: Overwinters in plant debris and spreads via splashing water and wind.

organic.safety: Do not apply during the hot afternoon sun.

chemical.safety: Wear protective clothing; check pre-harvest interval.

prevention: Keep potato plants well-fertilized to reduce stress.
Remove lower leaves that touch the soil.
Rotate fields out of Solanaceae crops.

name: Late Blight
symptoms: Dark, water-soaked spots on leaves expanding rapidly
White mold growth on the undersides of leaves in humid weather
cause: Oomycete pathogen thriving in cool, wet conditions.
lifecycle: Extremely fast-spreading spore cycles that can decimate a field in days.

organic.safety: Apply thoroughly to both sides of leaves.

chemical.safety: Ensure full coverage; wear respiratory mask during spray.

prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers.
Harvest only during dry weather to prevent tuber infection.
Destroy any volunteer potato plants immediately.

name: Healthy
symptoms: Vibrant green leaves with upright, healthy stems.
cause: High-quality soil, balanced fertilization, and good drainage.

prevention: Hill potatoes regularly to keep developing tubers covered.
Ensure consistent watering to prevent hollow heart.
Inspect weekly for Colorado potato beetles.

name: Blast
symptoms: Spindle-shaped (elliptical) lesions on leaves with gray centers
Brown borders around leaf lesions
Neck rot causing panicles to fall over
cause: Fungal infection favored by warm temp, high humidity, and high nitrogen fertilizer.
lifecycle: Spore release is highly correlated with night dews; overwintered straw is the host reservoir.

organic.safety: Apply under cloud cover or evening.

chemical.safety: Wear mask and goggles; check local crop safety instructions.

prevention: Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization.
Maintain proper water depth in the paddies.
Burn or deeply plow infected crop straw.

name: Healthy
symptoms: Evenly green, long upright leaves with healthy tillers.
cause: Optimum water level management, balanced NPK nutrients.
lifecycle: Normal reproductive and grain filling stages.

prevention: Perform systematic weeding.
Keep proper row spacing for sun penetration.
Monitor water quality and levels daily.

name: Rust
symptoms: Yellowish-orange powdery pustules arranged in stripes on leaves
Spore dust rubs off easily on fingers
cause: Fungal spores blown by wind across vast agricultural regions.
lifecycle: Complex multi-host cycle spreading rapidly during mild, wet spring weather.

organic.safety: Avoid skin contact; do not apply in high heat.

chemical.safety: Apply with clean sprayer; check pre-harvest safety interval.

prevention: Sow rust-resistant seed cultivars (crucial).
Plant early in the season to escape peak spore flights.
Remove host barberry bushes near fields.

name: Healthy
symptoms: Strong green leaves and stalks with well-formed wheat heads.
cause: Adequate soil nitrogen, cool spring weather, and clean seeds.

prevention: Maintain crop rotation schedules.
Avoid fields with previous heavy rust history.
Ensure uniform seeding depth.

name: Smut
symptoms: Large, white, fleshy galls on ears, tassels, or leaves
Galls rupture to release black, powdery spore masses
cause: Fungus entering the plant through wounds caused by insects or hail.
lifecycle: Spores survive in soil for years; wind carries them to damaged tissues.

organic.safety: Burn or bury galls far from the fields.

prevention: Select varieties with husk coverage resistance.
Avoid mechanical damage during cultivation.
Control stalk borers to prevent wound entry points.

name: Healthy
symptoms: Broad dark-green leaves, robust stalks, clean ears.
cause: Rich organic compost, standard moisture levels, no crop injury.

prevention: Rotate corn with soybeans or small grains.
Keep insect pest populations under control.
Optimize spacing to avoid high density humidity traps.
